text:
GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
nira Maintenance Therapy - Post Dose Reduction Follow-up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Marguerite Okonkwo is a 48-year-old Nigerian-American woman with FIGO stage IIIC, AJCC T3cN1M0 high-grade serous carcinoma of the ovary (BRCA1 germline pathogenic variant, c.5266dupC), presenting today for a scheduled follow-up visit approximately 6 weeks after reinitiation of nira at the reduced dose of 100 mg orally once daily, following a 4-week drug hold for Grade 2 thrombocytopenia.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Okonkwo was initially diagnosed with bilateral high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC, approximately 10 months ago. She underwent primary debulking surgery achieving complete gross resection (R0), with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, total abdominal hysterectomy, radical omentectomy, pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, appendectomy, sigmoid mesenteric implant resection, and right hemidiaphragm peritoneal stripping. Final pathology confirmed pT3cN1M0 disease with positive pelvic (4/38) and para-aortic (2/22) nodes; all resection margins negative. Her germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC, 5382insC founder mutation) was identified on Myriad myRisk panel, with confirmatory somatic tumor profiling (Foundation One CDx) showing BRCA1 somatic loss of heterozygosity, TP53 p.R175H, CCNE1 amplification, and an HRD score of 62 (positive).

Following surgical recovery, she completed 6 cycles of adjuvant carbo (AUC 6) and Taxol (175 mg/m2) every 21 days. Her course was notable for Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy beginning in cycle 2, which was ultimately managed conservatively and stabilized, and a Grade 2 Paraplatin hypersensitivity reaction during cycle 4 that was successfully managed with a 12-step desensitization protocol for cycles 5 and 6. She achieved CA-125 normalization (11 U/mL) at end of treatment with complete radiographic response on post-cycle 6 CT scan.

nira 200 mg daily (individualized starting dose based on body weight of 63 kg, below the 77 kg threshold) was initiated as first-line maintenance therapy approximately 8 months after diagnosis, given her complete response, germline BRCA1 status, and positive HRD score. Four weeks into Zejula, routine CBC revealed Grade 2 thrombocytopenia with a platelet count of 98,000/mcL. The drug was held; platelets recovered to 187,000/mcL within 2 weeks. Zejula was reinitiated at 100 mg daily (one dose level reduction), approximately 6 weeks ago. Bone marrow biopsy was deferred at that time given the highly typical clinical picture for PARP inhibitor-associated thrombocytopenia; peripheral smear showed no blasts or schistocytes, and workup for alternative etiologies (B12, folate, iron studies, reticulocyte count) was unremarkable.

Today she presents for evaluation of hematologic recovery, assessment of tolerability at the reduced nira dose, and overall disease surveillance.

She denies any bleeding symptoms - specifically no petechiae, no easy bruising, no gingival bleeding, no epistaxis, no hematuria, no melena, and no menorrhagia (she remains in surgical menopause). She denies abdominal distension, early satiety, pelvic pain, new back pain, urinary symptoms, or bowel habit changes. She reports no new lymphadenopathy. She specifically denies any symptoms that would raise concern for ovarian cancer recurrence.

She endorses mild fatigue, which she characterizes as Grade 1 - she is able to work full days and attributes it largely to the ongoing physiologic effects of Zejula rather than any functional limitation. She takes nira at bedtime with ondansetron 8 mg PO 30 minutes prior, which has significantly mitigated the nausea she experienced when first starting the drug. She describes nausea as Grade 1 - intermittent, not affecting her food intake, and not requiring additional antiemetics beyond the scheduled ondansetron.

She reports gradual improvement in her bilateral distal peripheral neuropathy, which was a residual complication from Taxol. She characterizes the current sensation as barely perceptible intermittent tingling in the toes bilaterally, which she rates as a 1 out of 10 in severity and states it does not interfere with daily activities, footwear, or ambulation. She notes with great satisfaction that her hair has fully regrown - she initially had Grade 2 alopecia with complete hair loss during chemotherapy, and regrowth has been progressing since completing pacli; she now has full-length hair.

She has returned to full-time work as a high school principal, which she finds fulfilling and reports to be a positive contributor to her psychological recovery and sense of normalcy.

On the psychosocial front, she continues to process the recent germline BRCA1 testing results for her children. Her older child (age 22) tested positive for the familial BRCA1 c.5266dupC variant and has been referred for enhanced surveillance (annual breast MRI, pelvic ultrasound, CA-125 monitoring, with plans for risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy at approximately age 35-40). Her younger child (age 19) tested negative. She has mixed emotions - relief for her younger child and appropriate concern for her older child. She is connected with oncology social work and finds it helpful.

Medications at today's visit:
- nira 100 mg PO once daily (bedtime)
- Ondansetron 8 mg PO 30 minutes before Zejula
- Levothyroxine 75 mcg PO daily (hypothyroidism)
- Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily (hypertension)
- Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO BID (initiated recently for iron deficiency)
- Calcium carbonate 600 mg + Vitamin D 400 IU, two tablets PO daily
- Acetyl-L-carnitine supplement (patient-initiated, for neuropathy support)
- Ondansetron 4 mg PO PRN additional nausea

Allergies: carbo (prior Grade 2 infusion hypersensitivity - managed with desensitization protocol; not a contraindication to re-exposure with protocol). No other known drug allergies.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Mild fatigue (Grade 1), no fever, no chills, no night sweats, no unintentional weight loss. She reports stable weight.
HEENT: No headache, no visual changes. Hair regrowth complete.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no lower extremity edema.
Pulmonary: No dyspnea at rest or on exertion, no cough.
Gastrointestinal: Mild intermittent nausea, Grade 1, controlled with bedtime ondansetron. No vomiting, no diarrhea, no constipation beyond mild change with iron supplementation, no blood in stool, no abdominal pain. No early satiety.
Genitourinary: No dysuria, no hematuria, no urinary frequency changes. In surgical menopause - no vaginal bleeding. Vaginal dryness managed with moisturizer.
Musculoskeletal: No new bone pain, no arthralgias, no myalgias. Neuropathic symptoms in toes as above (Grade 1).
Neurological: Peripheral neuropathy as above, improving. No cognitive changes, no headaches.
Skin: No rashes, no petechiae, no ecchymoses. Nails appear normal.
Psychiatric: Coping well with appropriate stress related to child's BRCA1 result. No significant depression or anxiety requiring escalation of support at this time. Social work follow-up ongoing.
Endocrine: Vasomotor symptoms of surgical menopause - mild to moderate hot flashes, managed non-hormonally.
Hematologic: No bleeding symptoms as above. No lymphadenopathy noted by patient.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Ms. Okonkwo is a well-appearing woman in no acute distress. She is alert, oriented, and conversational. She appears well-nourished with normal affect. Hair is present and appears fully regrown.

Vital Signs:
- Blood Pressure: 126/78 mmHg (left arm, seated) - well-controlled on amlodipine
- Heart Rate: 74 beats per minute, regular
- Respiratory Rate: 16 breaths per minute
- Temperature: 98.3 degrees F (oral)
- SpO2: 99% on room air
- Weight: 63.5 kg
- Height: 162 cm
- BMI: 24.2 kg/m2
- ECOG Performance Status: 0

HEAD, EYES, EARS, NOSE, THROAT: Normocephalic and atraumatic. Conjunctivae are pink without pallor (no clinical evidence of significant anemia). Sclerae anicteric. Oropharynx without mucositis or thrush. No cervical, supraclavicular, or submandibular lymphadenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. S1 and S2 without murmurs. No S3 or S4. No JVD. No peripheral edema bilaterally.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No dullness to percussion at the lung bases (no pleural effusion). No wheezing or rhonchi.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Bowel sounds present in all four quadrants. Midline laparotomy scar well-healed and non-tender. No hepatomegaly, no splenomegaly. No shifting dullness or fluid wave (no clinical ascites). No palpable masses or organomegaly.

Extremities: No peripheral edema. No calf tenderness or erythema. No petechiae or ecchymoses on the lower extremities.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 throughout. Gait normal. Light touch sensation: mildly diminished at distal toes bilaterally (consistent with residual Grade 1 Taxol-induced peripheral neuropathy); sensation normal at the ankle and above. Proprioception intact. Deep tendon reflexes 2+ at knees bilaterally, slightly diminished (1+) at Achilles bilaterally, consistent with known neuropathy.

Skin: No rash, no petechiae, no purpura. No ecchymoses beyond a small healing bruise on the right forearm (patient reports this is from bumping her arm at work; not out of proportion to trauma). Nails without paronychia.

Port-a-cath site (right anterior chest): Intact, non-erythematous, non-tender. No evidence of infection.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count (today):
- WBC: 4.8 x10^9/L (reference: 4.5-11.0) -- within normal limits
- ANC: 2.9 x10^9/L -- within normal limits, no neutropenia
- Hemoglobin: 11.4 g/dL (reference: 12.0-16.0) -- mildly below reference range (CTCAE Grade 1 anemia)
- MCV: 83 fL (reference: 80-100) -- normocytic
- Hematocrit: 34.1%
- Platelets: 156,000/mcL (reference: 150,000-400,000) -- within normal limits; CTCAE Grade 0 (fully recovered from prior Grade 2 thrombocytopenia)
- RDW: 14.2% (slightly elevated, consistent with iron repletion in progress)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L
- Potassium: 4.0 mEq/L
- Chloride: 103 mEq/L
- Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L
- BUN: 12 mg/dL
- Creatinine: 0.82 mg/dL -- stable, no nephrotoxicity
- eGFR: >60 mL/min/1.73m2
- Glucose: 94 mg/dL
- Total Bilirubin: 0.5 mg/dL
- AST: 22 U/L (normal)
- ALT: 18 U/L (normal)
- Alkaline Phosphatase: 67 U/L (normal)
- Total Protein: 6.9 g/dL
- Albumin: 4.0 g/dL

Tumor Markers:
- CA-125: 9 U/mL (reference: <35 U/mL) -- WITHIN NORMAL LIMITS; stable from prior value of 11 U/mL at end of chemotherapy
- HE4: 54 pmol/L (reference: <150 pmol/L premenopausal; <70 pmol/L postmenopausal -- within normal limits for a postmenopausal/post-oophorectomy patient)
- CEA: 1.8 ng/mL (within normal limits, sent for baseline)

Thyroid Studies (last checked 1 month ago, reported today):
- TSH: 2.1 mIU/L (within normal limits on current levothyroxine 75 mcg dose)

No imaging ordered today (patient is between scheduled surveillance CT scans; imaging is planned at 6-month intervals per surveillance protocol).

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Okonkwo is a 48-year-old woman with FIGO IIIC, T3cN1M0 high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma with germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC), currently on nira maintenance therapy at the reduced dose of 100 mg daily following prior Grade 2 thrombocytopenia requiring a 4-week drug hold. She is now approximately 6 weeks into the reduced dose and doing extremely well by all objective and subjective measures.

1. HIGH-GRADE SEROUS OVARIAN CARCINOMA - COMPLETE REMISSION, ON MAINTENANCE THERAPY

Ms. Okonkwo remains in complete clinical and biochemical remission. CA-125 is 9 U/mL (well within normal), HE4 is 54 pmol/L (normal), and she has no clinical or symptomatic evidence of disease recurrence. Her last CT scan (performed at end of chemotherapy) demonstrated complete radiographic response with no evidence of residual disease; next surveillance CT is scheduled in approximately 6 weeks as part of the 6-month surveillance imaging plan. The combination of R0 primary debulking surgery, complete response to platinum-based chemotherapy, BRCA1 germline pathogenic variant, positive HRD score (62), and CA-125 normalization places her in a highly favorable category for benefit from Zejula maintenance per the PRIMA/ENGOT-OV16 trial data.

Plan:
- Continue nira 100 mg PO daily (individualized reduced maintenance dose - no further dose reduction required given hematologic stability)
- Surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast and CT chest in approximately 6 weeks (scheduled)
- CA-125 and HE4 monitoring every 3 months
- Clinical examination every 3 months
- Reinforce recurrence awareness counseling: patient instructed to contact clinic promptly for abdominal distension, early satiety, pelvic pain, rising CA-125, or any new symptoms

2. Zejula-ASSOCIATED THROMBOCYTOPENIA - RESOLVED

Platelets have fully recovered to 156,000/mcL (Grade 0 per CTCAE v5.0), representing excellent hematologic recovery since the Grade 2 nadir at 98,000/mcL approximately 10 weeks ago. The patient has now been on Zejula 100 mg daily for 6 weeks without recurrence of significant thrombocytopenia. No bleeding symptoms were reported or identified on examination. The dose reduction from 200 mg to 100 mg daily appears to have been the appropriate management strategy, consistent with published data and the FDA label recommendation for Grade 2 thrombocytopenia. Per protocol, if thrombocytopenia were to recur at Grade 2 or above, a further dose reduction to 100 mg (already at this dose) would necessitate discontinuation per label; this was reviewed with the patient.

Plan:
- Continue Zejula 100 mg daily without further modification
- CBC with differential every 4 weeks throughout maintenance therapy
- Platelet counts should be monitored closely, particularly in the setting of any intercurrent illness, new medications, or anticipated changes that could affect bone marrow reserve
- Patient re-educated on signs of bleeding and instructed to call if platelet-related symptoms arise
- No bone marrow biopsy indicated at this time given resolved counts and clinical context

3. CHEMOTHERAPY-INDUCED PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY - IMPROVING, GRADE 1

Residual peripheral neuropathy from PTX is now at Grade 1 (barely perceptible distal toe tingling, no functional impairment). This is a significant improvement from the Grade 2 neuropathy experienced during treatment. Examination is consistent: mildly diminished light touch at the distal toes bilaterally, intact proprioception, symmetric findings. The natural history of pacli-induced neuropathy is gradual improvement over months to years following completion of the offending agent, and her trajectory is appropriately favorable. Zejula does not significantly contribute to peripheral neurotoxicity.

Plan:
- No pharmacologic intervention for neuropathy at Grade 1 (SNRIs, gabapentin, duloxetine deferred unless progression)
- Continue acetyl-L-carnitine per patient preference (evidence limited but low risk)
- Reassess at each visit; if progression to Grade 2, consider duloxetine 30 mg QD titrating to 60 mg
- Neurology referral if worsening or if functional impairment develops

4. MILD ANEMIA - STABLE, IRON DEFICIENCY COMPONENT

Hemoglobin today is 11.4 g/dL, which represents CTCAE Grade 1 anemia (10.0 g/dL to lower limit of normal). This is multifactorial: prior surgical blood loss (estimated 850 mL at debulking), chemotherapy-related erythropoietic suppression, iron deficiency (ferritin of 12 ng/mL, low serum iron, elevated TIBC identified on last CBC workup), and the known myelosuppressive effect of Zejula. Importantly, Hgb is 11.4 g/dL today, which represents a slight improvement from the prior value of 11.0 g/dL when iron supplementation was initiated, consistent with a response to oral iron. MCV is normocytic today at 83 fL, which may reflect mixed-deficiency picture with early iron repletion and concurrent Zejula effect; worth rechecking at next visit.

Plan:
- Continue ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO BID, taken with food to minimize GI intolerance (patient tolerating this approach)
- Recheck CBC with iron studies including ferritin, serum iron, TIBC in 4 weeks
- If Hgb falls below 8.0 g/dL (Grade 3 or severe Grade 2), would consider nira dose hold; currently no need for modification
- Erythropoiesis-stimulating agent not indicated at this time (Grade 1 anemia, asymptomatic, upward trajectory)
- No IV iron ordered at this time given upward hemoglobin trajectory on oral supplementation; will reassess

5. Zejula GI TOXICITY - MANAGED

The patient's nausea is Grade 1 and well-managed with scheduled bedtime ondansetron 8 mg PO 30 minutes prior to Zejula. This is the standard approach and is working effectively. No vomiting. Fatigue is Grade 1, not limiting function, and the patient is working full-time. These are expected and acceptable toxicities at the current reduced dose.

Plan:
- Continue ondansetron 8 mg PO 30 minutes pre-Zejula at bedtime
- PRN ondansetron 4 mg for breakthrough nausea
- Reassess at next visit; if nausea worsens, will consider adding metoclopramide or switching to palonosetron
- Fatigue counseling: light exercise (walking, yoga as tolerated) encouraged, consistent with ASCO guidelines showing benefit in cancer-related fatigue

6. SURGICAL MENOPAUSE MANAGEMENT

The patient underwent bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy as part of her debulking procedure and is in surgically induced menopause. She is managing vasomotor symptoms with non-hormonal approaches (previously discussed). Vaginal dryness is managed with vaginal moisturizer. She continues calcium 1200 mg + vitamin D 1000 IU daily for bone health; baseline DXA scan showed T-score of -1.2 (osteopenia) and she will have a follow-up DXA scan at 24 months from surgery.

Plan:
- Continue non-hormonal management of menopausal symptoms (given HGSOC diagnosis, hormone therapy is contraindicated)
- Calcium and vitamin D supplementation ongoing
- DXA scan at 24-month post-surgical mark (upcoming)
- If symptomatic worsening of vasomotor symptoms, can reassess SNRIs or consider referral to menopause specialist

7. PSYCHOSOCIAL AND FAMILY GENETIC COUNSELING

Ms. Okonkwo is coping well psychologically. She has returned to full-time work, which she finds meaningful and restorative. Ongoing emotional challenge relates to her older child's BRCA1 positive test result. She is appropriately processing this and is supported by the genetic counseling team and oncology social work. Her younger child tested negative, which has been a source of relief. She understands her own ongoing surveillance and maintenance plan well and is highly engaged in her care.

Plan:
- Maintain active connection with oncology social work
- Genetic counseling team managing enhanced surveillance planning for BRCA1-positive child (annual breast MRI from age 25, pelvic ultrasound and CA-125 monitoring, discussion of RRSO timing)
- Patient's own germline testing implications for extended family members discussed; she was encouraged to share results with siblings (she has two siblings) for their consideration of genetic testing

OVERALL IMPRESSION AND SUMMARY:

Ms. Okonkwo is doing remarkably well at this point in her course. She is in complete remission from her FIGO IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, tolerating Zejula maintenance therapy at the reduced dose of 100 mg daily without significant toxicity, has normalized CA-125 and HE4, is back at full-time work, and demonstrates no clinical or biochemical signs of disease recurrence. Her hematologic parameters are stable and recovering. The treatment course has been challenging but she has managed each complication expertly - from Paraplatin desensitization to nira dose reduction - and she is now on a stable, sustainable maintenance plan.

Return to clinic in 3 months (or sooner if any concerns arise). CBC monitoring in 4 weeks per protocol.

Time spent with patient: 45 minutes, greater than 50% counseling and coordination of care.

Electronically signed,
Attending Gynecologic Oncologist
Gynecologic Oncology Division
